name: Quintana del Castillo y San Feliz de las Lavanderas — circular por Astorga
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Quintana Del Castillo traverse a diverse landscape in the La Cepeda comarca of León, Spain. The region features mountainous terrain to the north, including Pico Pozofierro at 1,524 meters, and is situated at an altitude of approximately 1,016 meters, providing varied gradients. To the south, the Embalse de Villameca reservoir offers a serene backdrop, while oak and pine forests characterize much of the surrounding area. This combination of elevated areas, water features, and woodlands creates a varied environment for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Quintana Del Castillo The most popular touring cycling route is Quintana del Castillo y San Feliz de las Lavanderas — circular por Astorga, a 22.7 miles (36.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 48 minutes to complete. This route offers a circular journey through the local rural landscape. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Cerro Monteo – AP-66 Tunnel loop from Carrizo de la Ribera, a difficult 25.1 miles (40.5 km) path. This route explores the area around Cerro Monteo, featuring varied terrain and elevation changes. Local touring cyclists also love the Cerro Monteo loop from Carrizo de la Ribera, a 40.8 miles (65.7 km) trail leading through the region's hills and forests, often completed in about 4 hours 13 minutes. Touring cycling around Quintana Del Castillo is defined by mountainous terrain, the Villameca Reservoir, and extensive oak and pine forests. The network offers options for moderate and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 60 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Quintana Del Castillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Quintana Del Castillo?
A: There are over 10 touring cycling routes around Quintana Del Castillo, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. You'll find options ranging from moderate to difficult.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Quintana Del Castillo?
A: The region offers a diverse landscape for touring cyclists. You can expect mountainous terrain to the north, including Pico Pozofierro, and varied gradients as Quintana Del Castillo itself is situated at an altitude of approximately 1,016 meters. To the south, the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5551059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villameca Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; provides a different backdrop, while extensive oak and pine forests characterize much of the surrounding area.

Q: Are there routes suitable for experienced touring cyclists in Quintana Del Castillo?
A: Yes, for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 7 difficult routes available. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24567274" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Tesón loop from Brañuelas&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 69.3 km with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any easier touring cycling routes for beginners or those preferring a moderate pace?
A: Yes, there are 4 moderate touring cycling routes in the area. A good option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38171958" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Road Along the Porquera River – Villamejil Church Tower loop from Bar Matías&lt;/a&gt;, which is 33 km long and offers a more gentle experience.

Q: What natural features can I see while cycling in Quintana Del Castillo?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can cycle through oak and pine forests, observe diverse flora and fauna, and encounter significant water features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5551059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villameca Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1452688" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Órbigo&lt;/a&gt;. The mountainous terrain to the north also offers panoramic views.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks along the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area around Quintana Del Castillo has historical remnants. You might encounter Roman camps, old gold mining operations near the Villameca reservoir, and the architectural heritage of traditional Leonese villages. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1877639" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre de Ordás&lt;/a&gt; is one such historical site you could explore.

Q: Can I cycle near the Villameca Reservoir?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5551059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Villameca&lt;/a&gt; is a prominent natural feature to the south of Quintana Del Castillo. Its serene waters, surrounded by forests and dotted with ruins, provide a picturesque backdrop for cycling and are even suitable for bathing in the summer months.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, several routes are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1363131256/quintana-del-castillo-y-san-feliz-de-las-lavanderas-circular-por-astorga" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quintana del Castillo y San Feliz de las Lavanderas — circular por Astorga&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a journey through the local rural landscape.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Quintana Del Castillo?
A: The region's varied gradients and natural beauty make it suitable for cycling through much of the year. The summer months are particularly pleasant, with the option for bathing in the Villameca Reservoir. Spring and autumn offer milder temperatures and vibrant landscapes, ideal for exploring the oak and pine forests.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay near the cycling routes in Quintana Del Castillo?
A: Quintana Del Castillo and its surrounding villages retain a typical Leonese rural aesthetic, characterized by stone houses and traditional ovens. These charming villages often provide opportunities for stops, where you can find local eateries or accommodation, offering a glimpse into the local culture.

Q: What is the longest touring cycling route available?
A: The longest touring cycling route featured is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1276020" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Monteo loop from Carrizo de la Ribera&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 65.7 km. This trail leads through the region's hills and forests, typically taking around 4 hours and 13 minutes to complete.

Q: Can I find challenging routes with significant elevation gain?
A: Yes, the mountainous terrain ensures routes with notable elevation gain. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24567274" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Tesón loop from Brañuelas&lt;/a&gt; involves over 1300 meters of ascent, providing a demanding experience for those looking for significant climbs.
tips: Next to the Villameca reservoir is Quintana del Castillo, the head of its municipality and which, even though it was small, always played a role of some importance on the path of those who headed towards Astorga from the north. Little remains of the castle in its name, but its church is quite beautiful.

This is one of those many towns affected by depopulation but which, in turn, has made it remain almost the same as it was a century ago. With a beautiful church and two quiet and idyllic roads that lead to it, it is a good stopping point on your bike route.
